title: Arts of the Islamic World
description: Test your knowledge of the arts of the Islamic world!
topic_path: Art of the Islamic world 640 to now
Quiz — art of the Islamic world

assessment_items.question_content:
**Within the context of this tutorial, the term "Islamic Art" refers exclusively to religious art and architecture.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Within the context of this tutorial, "Islamic art" describes all of the arts that were produced in lands where Islam was the dominant religion or the religion of those who ruled.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/arts-of-the-islamic-world)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following religions claim Abraham as a key figure?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Abraham is a revered figure in Judaism, Christianity and Islam.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/introduction-to-islam)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following religions are monotheistic?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Judaism, Christianity and Islam are all monotheistic religions. Monotheism refers to religions where one God is worshipped; Polytheism refers to religions where more than one god is worshipped.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/introduction-to-islam)

assessment_items.question_content:
**On the Night Journey, the Prophet Muhammad traveled from Mecca to which city before ascending into heaven?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
According to Islamic tradition, the Prophet traveled during the Night Journey first to Jerusalem and from there ascended to Heaven.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/introduction-to-islam)

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to Islamic tradition, who led the Prophet on his famous Night Journey?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
According to Islamic tradition, the Angel Gabriel led the Prophet on the Night Journey.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/introduction-to-islam)

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Muslim calendar begins in the year 622 C.E. when the Prophet Muhammad and his followers fled Medina and were welcomed into the city of Mecca.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The Muslim calendar begins in the year 622 C.E. when the Prophet Muhammad and his followers fled Mecca and were welcomed into the city of Medina.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/introduction-to-islam)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Muslims believe that Muhammad was the final prophet in a line that included Moses, Abraham, and Jesus.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Muslims count Jesus among the prophets. 

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/introduction-to-islam)

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Hadiths are the saying of the Prophet, largely compiled in the centuries following his death.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The Hadiths (the sayings of the Prophet) are thought to have been compiled in the centuries following the Prophet's death.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/introduction-to-islam)

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Kaaba was originally constructed during the first years of Islam**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/74f6a9a78360fce54a0beedad53be433.jpeg)


The Kaaba, Mecca (photo: The Kaaba in the Masjid el Haram, 2010 Tab59 (CC BY-SA 2.0)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The Kaaba is believed to predate Islam.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/the-kaaba)

assessment_items.question_content:
**The holiest shrine in Islam is the $\_\_\_\_\_\_$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The Kaaba in Mecca is the holiest shrine in Islam.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/the-kaaba)

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Kaaba has undergone numerous modifications during its long history.**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/74f6a9a78360fce54a0beedad53be433.jpeg)
The Kaaba, Mecca (photo: The Kaaba in the Masjid el Haram, 2010 Tab59, CC BY-SA 2.0)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The Kaaba has been modified extensively throughout its history.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/the-kaaba)

assessment_items.question_content:
**The architecture and decorative forms found in the Dome of the Rock make reference to what other artistic traditions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The Dome of the Rock draws on both Byzantine and Sassanian art and architecture.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/early-period-the-dome-of-the-rock-qubbat-al-sakhra)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each image to the architectural feature it illustrates.**
[[☃ matcher 1]]
hints: A Sahn is courtyard.
A minaret is a spire or tower.

A mirhrab is a niche in the qibla indicating the direction of Mecca.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/introduction-to-mosque-architecture)

assessment_items.question_content:
**During the hajj, Muslims make a pilgrimage to $\_\_\_\_\_\_$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The hajj is a pilgrimage to the Kaaba in Mecca.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/the-five-pillars-of-islam)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Muslims pray in the direction of $\_\_\_\_\_\_$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The five pillars of Islam call for Muslims to pray towards Mecca five times each day.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/the-five-pillars-of-islam)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Muslims believe that they are meant to share their wealth with those who are less-fortunate in their religious community.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Giving alms, or giving to charity, is one of the Five Pillars of Islam.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/the-five-pillars-of-islam)

assessment_items.question_content: A [[☃ dropdown 1]] mosque, such as  [[☃ dropdown 2]]has an interior space supported by a forest of columns.
hints: The Great Mosque at Kairouan has an interior dense with columns which support the roof.

A hypostyle mosque typically has a roof supported by numerous columns.

[Read this to review](/humanities/art-history/art-history-400-1300-medieval---byzantine-eras/islamic-art/a/common-types-of-mosque-architecture)

assessment_items.question_content:
**The colors in Ottoman ceramic tiles helps us to date them. Put the following tile colors in chronological order.**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e731b1f506fdd370d5abcf541246960a.jpeg)
Tiles, Rustem Pasha Mosque


**Earliest:** [[☃ dropdown 1]]  
**Middle:** [[☃ dropdown 2]]  
**Latest:** [[☃ dropdown 3]]
hints: Ottoman tiles developed cobalt blue before 1525, then manganese purple, and then red around 1550.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Ottoman mosques often relied on endowments for their long-term financial maintenance. The Rüstem Pasha Mosque endowment is supported by income from $\_\_\_\_\_\_$.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The endowment for Rüstem Pasha Mosque is supported by the shops below.

choices.content: the rural estate of Rüstem Pasha

assessment_items.question_content:
**The decoration of the Damascus room (Qa'a) includes abstract geometric patterns rendered in sumptuous materials as well as poetic inscriptions all of which highlight the wealth and taste of the patron.**


![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b37901434afc3c193798797fdb7727d6.jpeg)

Reception Room (Qa'a), Ottoman period, 1119 a.h. / 1707 a.d., Damascus, Syria (Metropolitan Museum of Art)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Damascus Room features abstract geometric patterns which are typical of Islamic art and which reflect the wealth and taste of the patron.

assessment_items.question_content:
**The mihrab serves as a $\_\_\_\_\_\_$ **

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2ccb3de1135b5bd5c4ef47d523f43c62.jpeg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The mihrab is always on the qibla wall, which indicates the direction of Mecca.

choices.content: reminder of the direction of Mecca.
a place for particular religious rituals.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Muslims have always believed that God has prohibited all figurative art.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: There are many examples of figurative art in Islamic art history, though figurative art is normally reserved for secular spaces.

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Coronation Mantle was made by Norman artisans for the Muslim conqueror of Sicily.**
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/721a62877e312097d7eaed4204d4731b.jpeg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Coronation Mantle was made by Muslim artisans for Roger II, the Norman ruler who was Christian.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Locate the buildings below. It is possible to locate more than one building in a city.**

[[☃ categorizer 1]]
hints: The Kaaba in Mecca is the holiest shrine in Islam.
The Dome of the Rock in Jerusalem is one of the oldest surviving buildings from the Islamic World.
Rüstem Pasha Mosque and the Blue Mosque both resemble the Byzantine Church of Hagia Sophia—also in Istanbul.
The Great Mosque of Kairouan is in Tunisia.
The Great Mosque of Isfahan is in Iran.
